role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1111 Management Analysts 15.5% of wage bill + 13-1161 Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists 4.1% + 13-1199 Business Operations Specialists 2.9%; note oews_level is 4-digit 5416 proxy — for this code the true mix skews further toward marketing specialists and away from generic management analysts, plus a long tail of digital/content/brand specialists est. ~10-15% more
role_hints.note: Market research synthesis, competitive analysis, positioning decks, campaign performance reporting, content strategy drafts and persona work are heavily AI-exposed; client workshops, C-suite persuasion and judgment about what the client organization will actually execute persist. Plausible within-role fraction 0.3-0.5.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 13.5% + tail managers (11-2021 Marketing Managers and similar), est. ~4-6% more
role_hints.note: In a 49,047-firm industry where 41,556 firms have <5 employees, this line is substantially owner-operators; client relationships and selling persist, internal reporting and coordination automate.
Status reporting, scheduling and deliverable tracking automate substantially; client expectation management persists.

role_hints.role: Sales, business development and client service
role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3091 Sales Representatives of Services 3.6% + 43-4051 Customer Service Representatives 1.4%
role_hints.note: Proposal and pitch-deck production automates; relationship selling and referral networks do not.

role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1252 Software Developers 3.5% + analytics/data occupations in the tail, est. ~2-3% more
role_hints.note: AI-augmented; concentrated in larger firms doing martech implementation and analytics work.

role_hints.oews_basis: 13-2011 Accountants and Auditors 2.2% + 13-1071 HR Specialists 1.7% + office/admin long tail, est. ~8-12% more
role_hints.note: Invoicing, bookkeeping, scheduling, document production — the most directly automatable overhead.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in the listed top-10; the 4-digit top-10 covers only ~53% of the wage bill — remainder is writers, designers, digital specialists and misc. professional occupations
role_hints.note: Content and creative production lines are among the most AI-exposed tasks in marketing work; allocate explicitly rather than letting shares sum past 1.

source_hints.source: The CMO Survey (Duke Fuqua / Deloitte / American Marketing Association, semiannual)
source_hints.why: Longest-running marketing-leader survey; recent waves track AI use in marketing work — usable both for firm-side adoption and for the client-side bypass question.

source_hints.source: Salesforce State of Marketing / HubSpot State of Marketing reports (annual)
source_hints.why: Vendor surveys with AI-adoption rates among marketing practitioners; treat as directionally useful, vendor-biased.

source_hints.source: SPI Research Professional Services Maturity Benchmark (annual)
source_hints.why: Cross-professional-services data on billing models, realized rates and utilization — evidence for whether project fees are rescoped when hours fall.
Consulting-sector deal counts and EV/EBITDA multiples including marketing/digital consultancies — for active_consolidators and exit_mult.
Small-business sale multiples for the sub-$5M-revenue tier where most of this code's 49,047 firms sit; quoted multiples are of SDE — normalize.

source_hints.source: Exit Planning Institute State of Owner Readiness surveys
source_hints.why: Owner-age and succession-readiness data for small professional-services firms; check for a services-sector cut.

capture_questions: Marketing consulting bills as project fees and retainers built from day rates and estimated hours — when AI collapses delivery hours, do client procurement teams force rescoped fees at renewal, or can firms migrate to value-based/deliverable pricing and keep the spread? What share of engagements are already fixed-fee vs time-based?
Who owns the client relationship — the firm or the individual principal? In a market where 85% of firms have <5 employees, how much of a target's revenue survives the founder's exit?

What realistically stops a CMO from using ChatGPT-class tools plus internal staff for strategy, research and campaign planning — is there any evidence clients are already insourcing this work with AI?
Does proprietary data, vertical specialization or executional integration (martech stacks, media execution) create switching costs, or is the product a substitutable deck?
terminal_value_question: No license, statute or physical act protects marketing advice — the only mechanism is CMO accountability and the value of an outside perspective. When mature AI gives client-side teams instant strategy, research and creative, does paid third-party marketing consulting demand survive at all, or does AI replace the operator's product rather than cutting the operator's costs? Distinguish evidence of fee compression (cost cut passed on) from evidence of engagement volume decline (product replacement).
special_notes: Pricing-model deflation candidate: fees are anchored to estimated hours/day rates and renegotiated at renewal; client procurement benchmarking makes hourly-anchored retainers a direct deflation channel — model in pi_dist explicitly.

EBITDA normalization: the band population (1,936 of 49,047 firms) is dwarfed by a solo/micro tail; small-firm 'profit' is SDE with owner comp inside — normalize before comparing to any quoted multiples.

Boundary confusion: full-service and digital marketing agencies straddle 541613, 541810 (advertising agencies), 541511 (custom programming, for martech shops) and 541910 (market research); industry surveys branded 'marketing agencies' rarely map to this code cleanly — check whose members a benchmark covers.

Role-mix caveat: the record's role_mix is the 4-digit 5416 proxy (management consulting broadly); treat listed shares as approximate and expect this code to skew toward marketing specialists and creative/content occupations.
Key-person risk: client books are highly portable with individual consultants; earn-outs and non-competes matter more here than in licensed professions — probe in capture evidence.
